text: The surgeon sews the fascial defect in the midline, re-approximating the linea alba during an ETEP procedure.

The surgeon is suturing the fascial defect in the epigastric area using a needle driver in the right hand, positioned at an awkward angle from the left side, making it difficult to achieve perpendicular suturing to the linea alba.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument with the left hand, approaching the linea alba at a favorable angle, while carefully maneuvering the needle with the right hand to take precise bites despite the challenging position.

The surgeon adjusts the orientation of the surgical view by inverting the image, aiming to reassign the instruments for a more effective approach to the tissue, which is currently being engaged at a tangential angle.
The surgeon operates the fenestrated bipolar with the right hand and the needle driver with the left hand, achieving a better angle for suturing.

The surgeon uses a suture to sew the posterior rectus sheath edges together, running the suture back a few times. After completing the suturing, the surgeon trims the suture and adjusts the image orientation back to right side up.
